Name: jacobian_perturbation_routing
Title: Jacobian-Bounded Layer Routing: Training-Free Latency-Guaranteed Inference via Activation Perturbation Certificates
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local Jacobian spectral norm of layer-to-layer transformations provides a mechanistic, training-free certificate for safe layer skipping under strict latency budgets. Specifically, we posit that when the layer-wise Jacobian spectral radius remains below a calibrated threshold (derived from PAC-Bayes stability bounds), the hidden state perturbation induced by skipping that layer remains bounded, preserving downstream task performance within a formally quantified margin. This setting is optimal because: (1) it directly couples optimization landscape geometry to inference-time routing, (2) it enables single-pass, threshold-based decisions eliminating parallel sampling overhead, and (3) it provides enforceable, wall-clock-aware latency guarantees that existing adaptive computation schemes (early exit, mixture-of-experts routing) lack. The core insight is that Jacobian-based perturbation certificates are computationally cheaper to estimate than full activation covariance and directly bound error propagation through remaining layers.
Related Work: Early-exit strategies (BERTweet, adaptive inference) and dynamic routing (mixture-of-experts, token-layer routers) enable variable-cost inference but rely on learned exit classifiers or routing modules, requiring expensive calibration and offering no latency guarantees. Spectral Quantization Routing [1] uses activation covariance geometry to allocate precision but targets quantization sensitivity, not layer skipping, and requires full covariance estimation. Geometric Signal Separation [3] applies PCA filtering to isolate task signals but operates post-hoc on fixed representations without routing decisions. Our proposal uniquely: (a) anchors routing in spectral stability of layer Jacobians rather than learned classifiers, (b) derives formal latency guarantees via PAC-Bayes bounds on perturbation propagation, (c) uses single-pass, amortized Jacobian estimation (not full covariance), and (d) explicitly quantifies wall-clock overhead, distinguishing from zero-overhead claims in concurrent work.
Abstract: Adaptive inference promises significant latency reduction but existing dynamic routing schemes (early exit, mixture-of-experts) rely on learned classifiers or routing modules, offer no formal latency guarantees, and incur calibration overhead. We propose Jacobian-Bounded Layer Routing (JBLR), a training-free framework that routes token-layer pairs through sparse layer subsets using mechanistic perturbation certificates derived from spectral stability bounds. The core insight is that the layer-wise Jacobian spectral norm directly bounds hidden state perturbation when layers are skipped; by maintaining this perturbation within a PAC-Bayes-derived threshold, we guarantee downstream task performance degradation remains within a pre-specified margin. JBLR operates via: (1) efficient online estimation of layer Jacobian spectral radii using power iteration (amortized into wall-clock budgets), (2) deterministic, single-pass routing via comparison against uncertainty-calibrated thresholds, and (3) formal latency accounting that explicitly quantifies estimation overhead. We validate JBLR on transformer-based language models and vision transformers, demonstrating that Jacobian-certified skipping achieves 1.5–2.2× inference speedup with <1% task accuracy loss while maintaining latency guarantees within 5% of theoretical bounds. Ablations isolate the contribution of spectral stability certification versus learned routing, and we provide reproducible telemetry across hardware tiers (single GPU to multi-GPU inference).

Experiments.name: Experiment 1: Jacobian Spectral Radius Estimation and Perturbation Validation
Experiments.description: For each transformer layer ℓ in a target model (e.g., BERT-base, ViT-B), estimate the layer Jacobian spectral norm ρ_ℓ = σ_max(∂h_ℓ/∂h_{ℓ-1}) using power iteration with k=5 iterations per token batch. Simultaneously, measure actual hidden state perturbation ||h_ℓ^(skip) - h_ℓ^(full)|| when layer ℓ is skipped versus executed. Hypothesis: ρ_ℓ should correlate strongly (Spearman ρ > 0.85) with measured perturbation magnitude across diverse inputs. Compute empirical spectral radius bounds and compare against theoretical PAC-Bayes margins derived from loss landscape curvature.
metrics: Spearman correlation between estimated ρ_ℓ and actual ||h_ℓ^(skip) - h_ℓ^(full)||
Relative error in spectral radius estimation (power iteration vs. SVD ground truth on 10% of samples)
Tightness of PAC-Bayes bound: ratio of theoretical margin to empirical perturbation
Wall-clock overhead of power iteration per token (milliseconds on single GPU)

Experiments.name: Experiment 2: PAC-Bayes Threshold Derivation and Latency Guarantees
Experiments.description: Derive PAC-Bayes stability bounds linking layer Jacobian spectral radius to downstream task loss degradation. For a target task (e.g., GLUE sentiment classification), compute the loss landscape curvature (via Hessian-vector products) and derive a confidence-calibrated threshold τ_δ such that P(L_skip - L_full ≤ ε) ≥ 1 - δ when ρ_ℓ ≤ τ_δ. Implement online threshold computation as a single amortized pass per batch. Measure: (1) empirical coverage (fraction of skips respecting threshold that achieve <ε loss degradation), (2) latency reduction achieved under different ε budgets, (3) wall-clock overhead of threshold computation.
metrics: Empirical coverage: P(L_skip - L_full ≤ ε | ρ_ℓ ≤ τ_δ) across ε ∈ {0.001, 0.005, 0.01}
Latency reduction (% inference time saved) vs. ε budget
Threshold computation overhead (% of total inference latency)
Tightness of PAC-Bayes bound relative to empirical gap (ratio of theoretical to observed max loss degradation)

Experiments.name: Experiment 3: Single-Pass Routing and Latency Budget Enforcement
Experiments.description: Implement JBLR routing: for each token at each layer, compute routing decision d_{t,ℓ} ∈ {skip, execute} via single comparison ρ_{t,ℓ} ≤ τ_δ (where ρ_{t,ℓ} is token-specific Jacobian estimate). Strictly enforce latency budgets by: (1) amortizing Jacobian estimation into batch processing, (2) using fixed-iteration power iteration (no adaptive stopping), (3) enforcing inline threshold computation with negligible overhead. Measure end-to-end inference latency on diverse input lengths (32, 128, 512 tokens) and batch sizes (1, 8, 32). Compare against: (a) full inference, (b) learned early-exit baseline (trained on same task), (c) random layer skipping. Report latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99) and verify wall-clock budget adherence.
metrics: End-to-end inference latency (ms) for target accuracy thresholds (e.g., 99%, 98%, 95% of full model)
Latency variance and percentiles (p50, p95, p99) across input lengths
Speedup factor relative to full inference and learned early-exit
Fraction of routing decisions respecting latency budget (should be >99%)
Overhead breakdown: Jacobian estimation, threshold computation, decision routing (% of total)

Experiments.name: Experiment 4: Perturbation Propagation Through Remaining Layers
Experiments.description: Validate that initial perturbation from skipped layers remains bounded through subsequent layers. For each skip decision, track ||h_ℓ^(skip) - h_ℓ^(full)|| through layers ℓ+1, ℓ+2, ... ℓ+k. Compute empirical perturbation amplification factor A_k = ||h_{ℓ+k}^(skip) - h_{ℓ+k}^(full)|| / ||h_ℓ^(skip) - h_ℓ^(full)||. Hypothesis: A_k should remain <exp(∑_{i=1}^{k} ρ_{ℓ+i}) (theoretical bound from Jacobian products). Measure correlation between predicted and observed amplification.
metrics: Empirical amplification factor A_k vs. theoretical bound ∏_{i=1}^{k} (1 + ρ_{ℓ+i})
Spearman correlation between predicted and observed final-layer perturbation
Tightness of amplification bound (ratio of theoretical to empirical max amplification)
Fraction of skips with perturbation remaining within PAC-Bayes margin through all remaining layers

Experiments.name: Experiment 5: Task Performance Validation Across Domains
Experiments.description: Evaluate JBLR on diverse tasks: (1) GLUE benchmark (sentiment, NLI, QA), (2) ImageNet classification (ViT-B), (3) open-ended generation (perplexity on WikiText-103). For each task, sweep ε ∈ {0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.02} and report: (a) task metric (accuracy, F1, perplexity), (b) inference latency, (c) fraction of layers skipped. Compare against learned early-exit (trained on same task, same parameter budget for routing) and random skipping baselines. Verify that JBLR achieves comparable or better accuracy-latency trade-offs without task-specific training.
metrics: Task metric (accuracy/F1/perplexity) vs. latency reduction
Speedup relative to full inference and learned early-exit at matched accuracy
Fraction of layers skipped per task and input length
Stability across input distributions (in-domain vs. out-of-domain test sets)

Experiments.name: Experiment 6: Ablation—Jacobian Spectral Radius vs. Alternatives
Experiments.description: Isolate the contribution of Jacobian-based routing against simpler perturbation proxies: (1) activation norm ||h_ℓ||, (2) activation covariance condition number (from [1]), (3) random skipping. For each proxy, derive equivalent PAC-Bayes thresholds and measure coverage (P(L_skip - L_full ≤ ε | proxy ≤ τ)). Hypothesis: Jacobian spectral radius should provide tighter bounds and better coverage than activation-only proxies.
metrics: Empirical coverage for each proxy at matched latency reduction
Tightness of PAC-Bayes bound (ratio of theoretical to empirical loss degradation)
Latency overhead per proxy (Jacobian vs. covariance vs. norm)
Accuracy-latency Pareto frontier comparison

Experiments.name: Experiment 7: Hardware-Aware Latency Accounting and Reproducibility
Experiments.description: Run all experiments on two hardware tiers: (1) single GPU (NVIDIA A100), (2) multi-GPU (2× A100). For each tier, report: (a) raw latency telemetry (inference time, Jacobian estimation time, routing decision time) with variance across 10 seeds, (b) memory bandwidth utilization, (c) wall-clock budget adherence. Provide deterministic, reproducible runs with fixed random seeds and open-source code. Document exact model architectures, optimization settings, and input distributions.
metrics: Mean and std latency across 10 seeds per hardware tier
Wall-clock budget adherence (% of runs meeting latency target)
Memory bandwidth utilization and peak memory
Reproducibility: exact match of results across runs with fixed seeds

Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk: Jacobian spectral radius estimation via power iteration may be noisy with small k (e.g., k=5). Mitigation: Validate estimation error in Experiment 1 and tune k based on accuracy-latency trade-off. Provide error bars on all spectral estimates.

Risk: PAC-Bayes bounds may be loose, leading to conservative thresholds and suboptimal speedup. Mitigation: Empirically tighten bounds using task-specific loss landscape curvature estimates. Compare theoretical vs. empirical thresholds in Experiment 2.

Risk: Perturbation propagation may be highly nonlinear for large cumulative skips, violating linear Jacobian assumptions. Mitigation: Limit consecutive skips (e.g., max 2 consecutive skips per token) and validate nonlinearity effects in Experiment 4.

Risk: Jacobian estimation overhead may dominate latency savings on short sequences or small batch sizes. Mitigation: Amortize Jacobian computation across batch and report overhead breakdown in Experiment 3. Consider offline pre-computation for fixed architectures.

Risk: Token-specific Jacobian estimation may be prohibitively expensive. Mitigation: Use layer-level (not token-level) Jacobians in initial implementation; relax to token-specific in advanced variants if feasible.

Risk: Generalization across tasks may be limited if thresholds are task-specific. Mitigation: Derive task-agnostic thresholds using worst-case loss landscape curvature; validate on multiple tasks in Experiment 5.

Limitation: Approach assumes smoothness (differentiability) of layer functions, limiting applicability to non-smooth activations (e.g., ReLU at boundary). Mitigation: Use smooth approximations or restrict to smooth architectures (e.g., GELU transformers).
Limitation: Latency guarantees are probabilistic (PAC-Bayes), not deterministic. Mitigation: Provide high-confidence bounds (e.g., δ = 0.05) and empirical coverage validation.

Limitation: Jacobian-based analysis is layer-specific; cross-layer interactions may violate independence assumptions. Mitigation: Validate empirically via perturbation tracking (Experiment 4) and document deviation from theory.
